page: Amazon Web Services g4ad.xlarge is a graphics-focused virtual machine instance powered by the Nitro hypervisor. It features an AMD EPYC 7R32 processor with 4 vCPUs (2 physical cores with hyperthreading) running at 3.0 GHz, paired with 16.0 GB of DDR4 memory. For graphics acceleration, the instance includes a single AMD Radeon Pro Navi V520 GPU with 8 GB of VRAM. Local storage is provided via a 150 GB NVMe SSD instance store volume, and baseline network bandwidth is rated at 2 Gbps.

Benchmark results indicate that the CPU and memory performance are generally modest. Single-core and multi-core stress-ng tests place the instance in the bottom 10% of performance, while memory bandwidth tests fall into the bottom 25%. However, single-threaded tasks such as gzip compression and bzip3 decompression show average performance. Geekbench scores reflect average single-core capabilities but weak multi-core scaling. For specialized workloads, LLM text generation using a small 135M model achieves average speeds, though performance drops significantly with larger models.

The g4ad.xlarge offers a cost-efficient entry point for GPU-accelerated workloads that do not require high-end NVIDIA hardware, leveraging AMD's Radeon Pro architecture instead. The primary tradeoff is the lower CPU and memory throughput relative to compute-heavy instances. This configuration is best suited for graphics-intensive applications, basic game streaming, remote workstations, and light GPU-assisted workloads rather than heavy database operations or high-throughput web serving.
description: Amazon Web Services g4ad.xlarge is a graphics-focused virtual instance featuring an AMD EPYC 7R32 processor with 4 vCPUs and 16.0 GB of DDR4 memory. It is equipped with a dedicated AMD Radeon Pro Navi V520 GPU containing 8 GB of VRAM, alongside a 150 GB NVMe SSD local instance store. Operating on the Nitro hypervisor, the instance delivers a baseline network bandwidth of 2 Gbps. Benchmark data shows modest CPU and memory performance, with stress-ng and multi-core Geekbench results falling into the lower percentiles, while single-threaded compression tasks achieve average marks. This instance represents a qualitatively cost-effective option for workloads requiring hardware-accelerated graphics without the premium of high-end compute resources. It is suited for remote workstations, basic graphics rendering, and light GPU-assisted applications.
